text:
offshore 和 onshore 是英文中用来描述地理位置关系的两个词汇。它们通常用来区分一个实体位于国内还是海外。 offshore 的概念主要表示在海外或离岸的地区。例如，当留学澳大利亚的学生毕业后，选择留在澳大利亚并申请移民，我们称其为 onshore application。这里的 onshore 指的是在澳大利亚国内进行的 ...

Bonjour French friends,
Hereby I share my latest project: a 500mm long Mini Mono, based on John Finch Wildthing.
I share the drawing for free, and you can download it here:
https://drive.google.com/file/d/18h5v1BeNiVeqVx38g8DY5kYUVbUtM2Ao/view?usp=drivesdk
I started with sticking the drawing parts to the wood, for cutting the frames. You can use 2-3mm plywood:
I added 1mm thick birch plywood for the plating. No fibreglass, just glue:
Next to it's big brother, which I badly crashed previous season:
I added the servo tray and the motor. I start with a 28mm 2600kV Roxxy inrunner, which I still had:
I added a cheap rudder, with water cooling pickup. Basically, all parts I used are very cheap:
I made my own trim tabs and turn fins:
I made the cockpit from hard foam, laminated with 2 layers of glass weave:
Ready to run! It weighs just 873 grams including a 3S 3000mAh lipo:
Maiden run! It runs so great, I'm in love
😊
. It handles very good. It can take corners at full speed. The top speed is 53km/h with a 435 brass prop.
I made a 3-part video build series of this little boat. Here's the playlist, if you're interested:
RC Mini Mono build series:
My sons will enjoy this little boat now. Soon enough, I will start upgrading it
😎
. Adding a Rocket 2948 3450kV motor at first, running on 3S, and maybe try 4S. I would love to get 80km/h with this Mini Mono!

I'm now making a carbon hull. I made my own carbon plate from 2x 200gr/m2 weave. Laminated with 45 degrees angle between the weave strands.
The transom is still 3mm ply, so I can use inserts to install the hardware. I laminated the seams with a strip of weave, after gluing the hull together. It's very strong, and so far just 88 grams for this 50cm hull.

Je ne pense pas que cette coque soit faite pour des records de vitesse.
Elle semble ne pas avoir de caisson de retournement ce qui exclut les trop hautes vitesses.
Est ce une transmission immergée ou semi - immergée ?(la transmission sort-elle de la coque sous la coque ou sur le tableau arriere ?)
Je partirai sur une vitesse cible de 50 km/h et une helice K48 en semi immergée et K45 en immergé (même si ce n'est pas le top cela marche).
Une vitesse de rotation de 22 à 25 000 t/mn doit permettre d'atteindre cette vitesse.
Pour avoir de l'autonomie, les accus 4S que tu as sont pas mal. Il te faut un moteur type feigao 540L avec un Kv de 1700 à 1500. Le moteur serait monté sans reducteur.
Ces idées sont à debattre.

Une bonne nouvelle : ta transmission est semi immergée
Une mauvaise : elle n'est pas faite pour supporter beaucoup de puissance. Il ne faut pas investir trop dans cette coque. Tu peux essayer le config dont je t'ai parlée (K48 et moteur feigao de 1500 -1700 de kv) mais n'attends pas de miracles.
Après mon conseil serait de changer de coque. En sachant que le kv de 1500 à 1700 n'est pas adapté à du mono 2 qui est la coque standard de vitesse.
A toi de voir si tu veux investir dans cette coque (au maximum 50 km/h) ou passer sur un mono 2 pour aller vers 70 - 80 km/h.
En tout cas, n'utilise pas le moteur que tu as. Même à vide, la transmission par cardan n'y resistera pas.
A+
Pierre

J'iamgine que tu veux faire un bateau de vitesse. Oublions donc la coque, il faudra en acheter une nouvelle. Tu peux garder ta radio AM au debut si les deux voies sont proportionnelles, mais il faudra assez vite une radio FM.
Ensuite le moteur et les accus ne vont pas ensemble. Ton moteur est pour un mono 1 et l'accus est celui d'un mono 2. Le contro convient pour les deux (un peu juste pour le mono 2 dans une version un peu sportive ou si les caracteristiques du constructeur sont un peu optimistes.
Il faut choisir soit monter un mono 1 avec ton moteur et des accus 2S (en verifiant que ton controleur fonctionne en 2S) soit un mono 2 avec tes accus 4S.
Hors frais de port, tu peux trouver des moteurs à 50 ?. Le hardware varie entre 40 et 120 ? selon la qualité.
Une coque peut démarrer à 50 ? sur ebay et monter à 150 voir 200 chez un fabriquant de qualité
Si tu n'as pas de pb de budget, le mono 2 est plus gros, va plus vite et passe mieux les vagues mais c'est un peu plus cher.
Bonne réflexion !
A+
Pierre
PS : tu peux aussi choisr un acatamaran ou un hydro.

      Wind turbines  and  electrical substation  of  Alpha Ventus Offshore Wind Farm  in the  North Sea    
  Offshore wind power  or  offshore wind energy  is the  generation of electricity  through  wind farms  in bodies of water, usually at sea. Due to a lack of obstacles out at sea versus on land, higher wind speeds tend to be observed out at sea, which increases the amount of power that can be generated per wind turbine.   [ 1 ]    Offshore wind farms are also less controversial   [ 2 ]    than those on land, as they have less impact on people and the landscape.
  Unlike the typical use of the term "offshore" in the marine industry, offshore  wind power  includes inshore water areas such as lakes, fjords and sheltered coastal areas as well as deeper-water areas. Most offshore wind farms employ fixed-foundation wind turbines in relatively shallow water.  Floating wind turbines  for deeper waters are in an earlier phase of development and deployment.
  As of 2022, the total worldwide offshore wind power  nameplate capacity  was 64.3  gigawatt  (GW).   [ 3 ]     China  (49%), the  United Kingdom  (22%), and  Germany  (13%) account for more than 75% of the global installed capacity.   [ 3 ]    The 1.4 GW  Hornsea Project Two  in the  United Kingdom  was the world's largest offshore wind farm. Other large projects in the planning stage include  Dogger Bank  in the United Kingdom at 4.8 GW, and Greater Changhua in  Taiwan  at 2.4 GW.   [ 4 ]   
  The cost of offshore has historically been higher than that of onshore,   [ 5 ]    but costs decreased to $78/MWh in 2019.   [ 6 ]    Offshore wind power in Europe became price-competitive with conventional power sources in 2017.   [ 7 ]    Offshore wind generation grew at over 30 percent per year in the 2010s. As of 2020, offshore wind power had become a significant part of northern Europe power generation, though it remained less than 1 percent of overall world electricity generation.   [ 8 ]    A big advantage of offshore wind power compared to onshore wind power is the higher  capacity factor  meaning that an installation of given nameplate capacity will produce more electricity at a site with more consistent and stronger wind which is usually found offshore and only at very few specific points onshore.
 
 
  History   [   edit   ]   
  Capacity   [   edit   ]   
     An illustration of a hypothetical offshore wind farm in 1977  
 Europe is the world leader in offshore wind power, with the first offshore wind farm ( Vindeby ) being installed in  Denmark  in 1991.   [ 9 ]    
In 2009, the average  nameplate capacity  of an offshore wind turbine in Europe was about 3 MW, and the capacity of future turbines was expected to increase to 5 MW.   [ 9 ]   
  A 2013 review of the engineering aspects of turbines like the sizes used onshore, including the electrical connections and converters, considered that the industry had in general been overoptimistic about the benefits-to-costs ratio and concluded that the "offshore wind market doesn't look as if it is going to be big".   [ 10 ]      [ 11 ]   
In 2013, offshore wind power contributed to 1,567 MW of the total 11,159 MW of wind power capacity constructed that year.   [ 12 ]

 By January 2014, 69 offshore wind farms had been constructed in Europe with an average annual rated capacity of 482 MW.   [ 20 ]    
The total installed capacity of offshore wind farms in European waters reached 6,562 MW.   [ 20 ]   
The  United Kingdom  had by far the largest capacity with 3,681 MW. 
Denmark was second with 1,271 MW installed and Belgium was third with 571 MW. 
Germany came fourth with 520 MW, followed by the Netherlands (247 MW), Sweden (212 MW), Finland (26 MW), Ireland (25 MW), Spain (5 MW), Norway (2 MW) and Portugal (2 MW).   [ 20 ]   
  At the end of 2015, 3,230 turbines at 84 offshore wind farms across 11 European countries had been installed and grid-connected, making a total capacity of 11,027 MW.   [ 21 ]      [ 22 ]   
The history of the development of wind farms in the North Sea, as regards the United Kingdom, indicates three phases: coastal, off-coastal and deep offshore in the period 2004 through to 2021.   [ 23 ]    Through the development of offshore wind power the  Baltic Sea  is expected to become a major source of energy for countries in the region. According to the Marienborg Declaration, signed in 2022, all EU Baltic Sea states have announced their intentions to have 19.6 gigawatts of offshore wind in operation by 2030.   [ 24 ]   
  Outside of Europe, the Chinese government had set ambitious targets of 5 GW of installed offshore wind capacity by 2015 and 30 GW by 2020 that would eclipse capacity in other countries. 
However, in May 2014 the capacity of  offshore wind power in China  was only 565 MW.   [ 25 ]    
Offshore capacity in China increased by 832 MW in 2016, of which 636 MW were made in China.   [ 26 ]   
  The offshore wind construction market remains quite concentrated.
By the end of 2015,  Siemens Wind Power  had installed 63% of the world's 11 GW   [ 27 ]    offshore wind power capacity;  Vestas  had 19%,  Senvion  came third with 8% and  Adwen  6%.   [ 28 ]      [ 16 ]   
About 12 GW of offshore wind power capacity was operational, mainly in Northern Europe, with 3,755 MW of that coming online during 2015.   [ 29 ]    As of 2020 90% of the offshore global market was represented by European companies.   [ 30 ]   
  By 2017, the installed offshore wind power capacity worldwide was 20 GW.   [ 31 ]    In 2018, offshore wind provided just 0.3% of the global electricity supply.   [ 32 ]    Nevertheless, just in 2018 an additional amount of 4.3 GW of offshore wind capacity was employed on a worldwide scale.   [ 32 ]    In Denmark, 50% of the electricity was supplied by wind energy in 2018 out of which 15% was offshore.   [ 33 ]    The average size of turbines installed was 6.8 MW in 2018, 7.2 MW in 2019 and 8.2 MW in 2020.   [ 34 ]   
  In 2022, the offshore wind industry marked its second-largest yearly growth, adding 8.8 GW and increasing global capacity to 64.3 GW—a 16% rise from the previous year. The  Global Wind Energy Council  (GWEC) anticipates a significant expansion, projecting an additional 380 GW by 2032 to reach a total of 447 GW. However, market challenges in Europe and the US could slow progress, with only a third of the anticipated capacity expected to be installed between 2023 and 2027.   [ 35 ]   
 
  Costs   [   edit   ]   
 In 2010, the US Energy Information Agency said "offshore wind power is the most expensive energy generating technology being considered for large scale deployment".   [ 5 ]   
The 2010 state of offshore wind power presented economic challenges significantly greater than onshore systems, with prices in the range of 2.5-3.0 million Euro/MW.   [ 36 ]    
That year,  Siemens  and  Vestas  were turbine suppliers for 90% of offshore wind power, while  Ørsted A/S  (then named DONG Energy),  Vattenfall  and  E.on  were the leading offshore operators.   [ 1 ]   
  In 2011, Ørsted estimated that while offshore wind turbines were not yet competitive with fossil fuels, they would be in 15 years. 
Until then, state funding and pension funds would be needed.   [ 37 ]    
At the end of 2011, there were 53 European offshore wind farms in waters off Belgium, Denmark, Finland, Germany, Ireland, the Netherlands, Norway, Sweden and the United Kingdom, with an operating capacity of 3,813 MW,   [ 38 ]    while 5,603 MW was under construction.   [ 39 ]   
Offshore wind farms worth €8.5 billion ($11.4 billion) were under construction in European waters in 2011.   [ 40 ]   
  In 2012,  Bloomberg  estimated that energy from offshore wind turbines cost  €161  ( US$208 ) per MWh.   [ 41 ]   
  Costs of offshore wind power are decreasing much faster than expected. 
By 2016, four contracts ( Borssele  and  Kriegers ) were already below the lowest of the predicted 2050 prices.   [ 42 ]      [ 43 ]
